blocks.statement: I can identify magnetic poles (N + S), describe magnetic field lines (out of N, into S), and explain why Earth itself is a giant magnet — the magnetosphere generated by Earth's iron core dynamo.

blocks.whatWeKnow: Static charges (Day 1-3) make electric fields. Now: moving charges + magnets make MAGNETIC fields.
blocks.connection: Today previews Day 16: Earth's magnetosphere — the magnetic shield generated by THIS physics — is what made life on Earth possible.

blocks.markdown:
## A second kind of field

Unit 7 started with the electric field E: a vector at every point in space, sourced by charges. Magnetism gives us a second field, **B**, with its own rules:

- Every magnet has TWO poles, **N and S**. Field lines come **out of N, into S**.
- **Unlike poles attract; like poles repel** — the same attract/repel logic as charge, but with a twist: you can never isolate one pole. Cut a magnet in half and you get two smaller complete magnets.
- B is measured in **tesla (T)** — a huge unit. A fridge magnet is ~0.1 T at its surface; an MRI machine is 1–3 T; Earth's field is a tiny **25–65 μT**.

And the big one: **Earth itself is a magnet.** Molten iron circulating in the outer core — the **dynamo** — generates a planet-sized field. That field is why a compass works: the needle's N end aligns with Earth's field and swings toward geographic North. Small in teslas, planetary in consequence — Day 16 shows what it protects us from.

terms.definition: The two ends of a magnet. North poles attract South poles; like poles repel. Free poles ALONE don't exist — cut a magnet, you get two smaller bipolar magnets. Example: bar magnet, refrigerator magnet, compass needle — all have N + S. The Earth's geographic NORTH is actually a MAGNETIC SOUTH (a compass N points to it).
terms.cognate: Sp. polos magnéticos · Pt. polos magnéticos · HC pòl mayetik

terms.definition: A vector at every point in space. Drawn as field lines: out of N, into S. Units: tesla (T) — very large. Example: a neodymium fridge magnet: B ≈ 0.1 T at the surface. Earth's field: B ≈ 25–65 μT. An MRI machine: B ≈ 1–3 T (HUGE). B is to magnetism what E is to electricity — but B comes from MOVING charges or magnetic materials, E from static charges.
terms.cognate: Sp. campo magnético · Pt. campo magnético · HC chan mayetik

terms.definition: Earth has a magnetic field generated by the molten iron flow ('dynamo') in its outer core. Field strength: 25–65 μT at the surface. Polarity REVERSES on geologic timescales. Example: the field that makes a compass work — N end of the needle aligns with Earth's magnetic field, pointing to (geographic) North.
terms.cognate: Sp. campo magnético terrestre · Pt. campo magnético da Terra · HC chan mayetik latè

blocks.title: No monopoles — and North isn't (magnetically) north
blocks.markdown: No 'magnetic monopoles' exist in nature (so far). Poles always come in pairs — different from electric charges, where a single + or − exists on its own. And watch the map: geographic N pole ≠ magnetic N pole. They're tilted ≈ 11° apart, and a compass needle's N points to Earth's magnetic SOUTH (which is near geographic NORTH).

blocks.instruction: Bar magnet with field lines (out of N, into S). Earth as a giant bar magnet — show the tilt between magnetic and geographic axes. Mark where a compass would point.
prompts: Bar magnet: field lines out of N, into S.
Earth as a bar magnet, tilted ≈ 11° from the spin axis.
Mark which way a compass needle points.

blocks.prompt: FIELD STRENGTH COMPARISON — An MRI machine has B ≈ 1.5 T. Earth's field is ≈ 50 μT. How many TIMES stronger is the MRI field?
blocks.answer: MRI is 30 000 times stronger than Earth's field.

blocks.prompt: COMPASS DIRECTION — A compass needle settles pointing approximately to geographic North. Which POLE (N or S) of the needle is pointing 'up' (toward geographic N)? Why?
blocks.givenHint: The needle aligns with Earth's field. Earth's magnetic pole near geographic North is actually a magnetic SOUTH.
blocks.equationHint: No numbers — use the pole rule: unlike poles attract.
equationOptions: Unlike poles attract; like poles repel
Like poles attract; unlike poles repel

blocks.title: ★ 50 μT — small field, planetary job
blocks.markdown: Earth's iron-core dynamo generates ≈ 50 μT — small in absolute terms, but it deflects most of the solar wind (charged particles at 400 km/s) before they hit our atmosphere. Without it, the atmosphere would have eroded billions of years ago — and you wouldn't exist. (Day 16.)

blocks.prompt: What's the source of Earth's magnetic field? Answer in one sentence using the word 'dynamo'.
blocks.frame: Magnetic poles come in ___ — N and S. Like poles ___; unlike poles ___. Earth's magnetic field is generated by the ___ at the core, with strength ≈ ___ μT.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **36.1–36.4 and 36.9** (magnetic poles, fields, the nature of magnetism, domains, and Earth's magnetic field). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
